texts.label: text
texts.orig: The  ability of  thin  composite  membranes to  separate  dissolved  organic  solvents  from water by pervaporation has been examined.  The  separation performance  of  a pervaporation process was considered as a combination  of an evaporation step  and a diffusion  step.  For the experimental work, poly (dimethylsiloxane) and polyolefin  composite  membranes were incorporated into  O.l0.3  m2, 2-inch diameter laboratory  spiral-wound test  modules. Typical fluxes  were in  the range 0.2-1.5  L/m2-h,  depending  on the operating conditions and the feed solution. The  membranes used in this  work  were non-selective  for  polar  solvents  such as  ethanol  (a,,,<  1) so that the overall  enrichment of polar solvents  in the permeate  was  typically  5-10 fold.  With  non-polar solvents  such as  ethyl  acetate, chloroform and 1,1,2-trichloroethane, where  the membrane selectivity, amem, is  greater  than I,  organic  enrichment  in  the  permeate  of  50-400 fold  was obtained. Pervaporation therefore  appears to  be an economically viable  method  of  removing  non-polar solvents from dilute aqueous streams.  Preliminary calculations show  plant  capital costs  in  the  range $2-5/gal of  feed  water per  day and operating  costs  of  $2-lO/l,OOO gal  of  feed  water treated.

texts.orig: Pervaporation  is a membrane process which can be used to separate volatile dissolved  organics  from  liquid mixtures.  The  pervaporation  process  is shown schematically  in Fig. 1. A liquid mixture contacts  one side of a membrane; the permeate  is removed  as a vapor  from  the  other  side. Transport  through  the membrane  is induced  by  maintaining  the  vapor  partial pressure on  the permeate side lower than the partial pressure on the liquid side. This partial pressure difference  can  be  achieved  in several ways,  most economically  by  developing a moderate vacuum on the permeate side of the membrane through cooling and condensing  the permeate vapor. Water and organic solvents permeate the membrane at a rate determined by their relative volatilities and their intrinsic permeabilities  through  the membrane.  The  process  has elements  in common with air and steam stripping, in that the more volatile contaminants  are preferentially  concentrated  in the permeate. texts.orig: The  origins of pervaporation  can be traced to the 19th century, but the first systematic studies were done in the late 1950s [ 11. The process did not become commercial at that time. The problems that inhibited development in the 1960s were the  low  selectivities  and  low  fluxes  of  available  membranes.  With  advances in membrane making technology,  interest in pervaporation  has revived, principally  through  the work  of  Aptel  and  Neel  [2],  and  Tusel,  whose  company,  GFT,  introduced  the  first  commercial  alcohol  dehydration  pervaporation  plant  in  1982  [3,  41.  During  the  past  four  years,  we  have  conducted  a research program  aimed  at developing  high-flux,  high-selectivity  pervaporation  membranes.  The  basis of our approach  has been the preparation  of thinfilm composite  membranes.  These  membranes  have been tested in laboratory test cells and in spiral-wound  membrane  modules.  In our work, we have concentrated  on  the  separation  of  low  concentrations of  organic  solvents  from water. The  results are promising  and these  systems are now  being  produced commercially (PerVap  Systems,  Membrane  Technology and  Research,  Inc., Menlo  Park, CA).

texts.orig: The  concentrations  of the components  i and j within the membrane at each interface  will be proportional  to the chemical  potentials  of components  i and j in the  adjacent  fluids.  We  can  express  this  chemical  potential  by  the partial vapor  pressures, pi and pj.  On the  feed  side of  the  membrane,  for  a solution containing  components  i and j at the mole concentrations  ci and cj, the chemical potential  can be expressed as the partial pressure of the vapor  in equilibrium with the feed solution.  On the permeate side of the membrane, the chemical potential  is the vapor  pressure  of  i and j  in the permeate  gas stream. This follows directly from eqns. (5) and (6))  which show that the flux through  the membrane  is proportional  to the equilibrium vapor pressure of  components  i and j  in the feed  liquid.  Increasing  the  hydrostatic pressure of this liquid has a negligible  effect  on  the  feed  vapor  pressure and, thus, on the trans-membrane  flux and the selectivity.

texts.orig: low  for  commercial  use.  Commercial  systems,  thus  far,  have  used thin-film composite  or asymmetric  membranes  in which  a thin permselective  skin, 0.22 pm thick,  is supported  on  a microporous  support structure. In our work, we used  composite  membranes  made  by  coating  a relatively  open  microporous support  membrane  with  a thin,  dense  film.  The  two  types  of  membrane  we have used are shown schematically  in Fig. 3. One membrane consists of a single poly (dimethylsiloxane ) layer deposited  on the microporous  support. The  second is a multilayer structure consisting of a poly (dimethylsiloxane) layer overcoated  with  a second  permselective  polyolefin  layer. The  olefin  layer  is less permeable than the poly (dimethylsiloxane),  but very selective, so that the bulk of the resistance to permeation  lies in the olefin  coating.  In this case, the role of the poly (dimethylsiloxane) layer is to provide  a smooth,  defect-free  surface on  which  the  olefin  film  can  be deposited.  Both  types  of  membrane  were incorporated  into simple spiral-wound  membrane  modules.

Commercial  systems,  thus  far,  have  used thin-film composite  or asymmetric  membranes  in which  a thin permselective  skin, 0.22 pm thick,  is supported  on  a microporous  support structure. In our work, we used  composite  membranes  made  by  coating  a relatively  open  microporous support  membrane  with  a thin,  dense  film.  The  two  types  of  membrane  we have used are shown schematically  in Fig. 3. One membrane consists of a single poly (dimethylsiloxane ) layer deposited  on the microporous  support. The  second is a multilayer structure consisting of a poly (dimethylsiloxane) layer overcoated  with  a second  permselective  polyolefin  layer. The  olefin  layer  is less permeable than the poly (dimethylsiloxane),  but very selective, so that the bulk of the resistance to permeation  lies in the olefin  coating.  In this case, the role of the poly (dimethylsiloxane) layer is to provide  a smooth,  defect-free  surface on  which  the  olefin  film  can  be deposited.  Both  types  of  membrane  were incorporated  into simple spiral-wound  membrane  modules.

texts.orig: Composite  membranes  used in this research were prepared  by  a multistep process. First, a microporous  support film was formed on a polyester  fabric. In a second step, the microporous support was coated with a thin poly (dimethylsiloxane) layer. For some experiments,  a third highly  selective material, such as polyolefin,  was coated on the poly (dimethylsiloxane ) layer.

texts.orig: The  thickness  of  the  permselective  layer  of  a composite  membrane  is obtained by comparing  its nitrogen flux with values obtained  with thick isotropic films of the permselective  material. The presence of membrane defects can also be  estimated  by  measuring  the  oxygen  and  nitrogen  permeability  ratio.  We considered the poly (dimethylsiloxane) layer defect-free  if the permeability  ratio was 2.1 or more.  We  found  that the thickness  and number  of  defects  in a membrane  (as  measured  by  the  0,/N, permeability  ratio)  vary  widely  depending  on  the  concentration and  viscosity  of  the  coating  solvent  used,  the method  of  applying  the  coating  solution,  and  the  properties  of  the  support membrane.  Currently, the thinnest  defect-free  poly (dimethylsiloxane) membrane that can be made on a polysulfone  support by our technique  is approximately 0.5 pm. Thinner  membranes were made, but yielded 0,/N, permeability ratios less than 2.1, which may indicate the formation  of membrane defects.

texts.orig: The  small laboratory  test loop  used in these experiments  required 5-20  L of feed  solution  to  operate.  The  test  unit  is shown  schematically  in Fig. 4. The small feed pump  was used to recirculate  solution  from  the feed tank through the test module; a vacuum pump was used to produce the low pressure required on the permeate side of the membrane.  The condenser  system consisted of two traps that could  be used alternately,  allowing the permeate  stream to be sampled  continuously without  interrupting  operation  of  the  unit.  Feed  solution samples and permeate samples were analyzed by gas chromatography  (GC)  or high-pressure  liquid chromatography  (HPLC ) .

texts.orig: In  preliminary  experiments,  we measured the  effect  of  permeate  pressure and feed solution temperature on system performance  using pure water as the feed  solution.  The  results are shown  in Fig 5. In these experiments,  the permeate pressure was varied and the permeate flux measured. As expected  from eqn.  (5), the  flux  decreases  linearly  with  increasing  permeate  pressure  and reaches zero when the permeate pressure equals the vapor pressure of the feed solution.  The  flux versus permeate pressure lines shown in Fig. 5 are also parallel to each other. This  implies that the membrane permeability,  i.e., flux per unit vapor pressure driving force,  is independent  of temperature  in the range of  25-45°C.

texts.orig: The  effect  of permeate pressure on flux at a constant  temperature is shown in Fig. 6. As the permeate pressure increases and the difference  in partial pressure between the feed and permeate sides of the membrane decreases, the flux also  decreases.  The  flux  is zero  when  the  permeate  pressure  equals the  feed solution partial pressure. Figures 6 and 7 also show that the total permeate flux increases almost linearly with increasing alcohol  concentration  in the feed solution. The increase is principally  due to the increasing alcohol flux. The water flux remains relatively constant  and independent  of the ethanol concentration in the feed. These  results are in complete  accord  wit eqns. (5  ) and  (6 ) .

texts.orig: In this economic  analysis, two potential  applications  of pervaporation  were considered.  The  first is the removal of small amounts  of organic solvents such as 1,1,2-trichloroethane from  water.  In this  case, the  objective  is to  solve  an environmental  pollution  problem.  At the present  time, the competitive  technologies  are carbon  adsorption  and air stripping. Typical  feed streams in this case contain  10 to  1,000 ppm  solvent.  The  second  application  is the  recovery of  dissolved  organic  solvents  from  industrial  process  streams.  The  objective here is to recover a valuable product.  The competitive  technologies  are solvent extraction  and distillation.  Typical  feed  streams in this case contain  from  l5% solvent.

texts.orig: As  the  base-case  for  an  analysis  of  solvent  removal  from  a contaminated water stream, we considered  a system designed to treat 100,000 gallons of feed water per day, containing  0.1 wt%  of  dissolved  organic  solvent.  We  assumed that  the  membrane  removes  90%  of  this  dissolved  solvent  to  produce  an  effluent  stream containing  approximately  0.01 wt%  solvent.  Based on our laboratory work, an average membrane water flux of 0.5 L/m'-hr and a separation factor  for to the dissolved  organics  over water of 200 are reasonable.

texts.orig: To  calculate  the pervaporation  system's performance,  a computer  program using crossflow permeation  equations was used. In this model, the feed solution flows parallel to the membrane and permeate vapor flows perpendicular to and away from  the  membrane.  Spiral-wound  membrane  modules  operate  in this crossflow pattern. The  computer program treats the system as a series of wellmixed elements. The permeate stream composition  and permeate volume flow are calculated  for  the  first  individual  element.  The  mass balance  then  yields the feed stream composition  of the second element. This procedure  is repeated until the desired residue composition  is obtained.  The  overall permeate  composition  is obtained  by averaging the permeate composition  of the elements. A minimum of 50 elements was used in each calculation.

texts.orig: No  charge was  taken for  disposal of  the 1,240 gal/day of  7.4% solvent solution  produced by  the  plant. Depending on  the solvent of  the  flow,  recovery for re-use may  be  economically more attractive than simple incineration. Re-use would  certainly be  an  attractive possibility if  the  solvent is  partially soluble in water, such  that phase splitting of  the  permeate occurs. If  recovery were  possible, a  credit of  $100 to  $200/day could be  taken, significantly reducing the operating costs. If  on-site incineration were  used, the cost of  disposal would probably be  about equal to  the fuel value. If  off-site disposal had  to  be  used, then additional concentration, perhaps by  a  second pervaporation unit,  would be  worthwhile.

texts.orig: Figure  19 illustrates the  effect  of  membrane  flux  and  separation  factor  on performance.  As shown,  costs  decrease dramatically  as either the membrane flux  or the  separation  factor  increase.  Increases  in separation  factor  also  increase the concentration  of solvent in the permeate stream, making final treatment of this stream much easier. However,  even a membrane with very modest (  N 20)  separation  factor  may be useful,  for  example,  in a pretreatment  plant concentrating  several types  of polluted  streams. The  cost  of  a pervaporation solvent removal system will be very sensitive to the flux and separation factor of a particular membrane for a particular solvent. Because membrane flux usually  falls  as separation  factor  increases,  these  two  factors  must be  balanced. Figure 20 shows the  effect  of  these  factors  on  system  performance,  together with the  actual  data points  obtained  with  poly (dimethylsiloxane) and polyolefin  membranes  and  1,1,2-trichloroethane solution.  The  separation  factors observed in  this  case  are  300  for  poly(dimethylsiloxane) and  1,000  for polyolefin.

texts.orig: In the base-case  calculations,  90%  solvent  removal was assumed, although, in practice,  there  will be circumstances  in which  greater removal  is required. Higher  fractional  solvent  removal  can be achieved,  but,  as with flux  and separation factor,  this involves  a trade-off  between  one performance  characteristic and another. As shown in Fig. 21, the solvent concentration  in the permeate stream decreases with increasing  fractional  solvent  removal,  and this in turn will increase  subsequent  disposal  or treatments  costs.  However,  our  calculations  showed that permeate concentrations  can be maintained above 2%, even at 99% solvent removal from the feed, provided the membrane has a separation factor  greater than  100. In this type  of  high  solvent  removal  application,  the more selective polyolefin  membrane  may be preferred.

texts.orig: As the base case for  an analysis of  solvent  recovery  from an industrial process stream, we considered  a system designed to treat 100,000 gallons of feed water per day, containing  2.0 wt%  of  dissolved  organic  solvent.  We  assumed that  the  membrane  treatment  removes  90%  solvent  to  produce  an  effluent stream containing  0.2 wt%  solvent.  At  this  solvent  concentration, the  flux through the membrane is directly proportional  to the solvent content.  For the base case, we assumed a transmembrane  pure water  flux  of  0.5 L/m'-hr increasing linearly with solvent concentration  up to 1 L/m2-hr  at 2 wt% solvent. The  membrane selectivity was taken to be  100. All of these values are justifiable based on the experimental  data obtained.

Combining  these with the cost assumptions  discussed above yielded the capital and operating costs of the system. The costs are summarized for the base case in Tables  6 and 7.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Both  capital  and  operating  costs  for this  solvent  recovery  plant  are two to three  times  the  equivalent  costs  for  the  pollution  control  plant  designed  to treat the same feed flow. On the other hand, this type of plant could produce  a permeate  stream of 4,000 gallons/day containing  approximately  46%  organic solvent. For its fuel value alone, this stream is worth almost $l,OOO/day or $lO/ 1,000 gallons of feed solution  treated. The value as purified,  recovered  solvent may be even greater.

texts.orig: In many plants, 90% recovery  of the organic solvent content  of the feed may not be optimum. For example, in a fermentation  process, 30-50%  recovery may suffice and the retentate can be recirculated back to the fermentation  tank. As with the pollution  control  example, a fractional  recovery greater than 90% may be  necessary  if  the  ultimate  fate  of  the  retentate  stream  is discharge  to  the sewer. texts.orig: This program demonstrated the utility of pervaporation  as a new and potentially economical  method  of recovering  low concentrations  of organic solvents from  aqueous  streams. Pervaporation  units can be tailored for pollution  control or solvent  recovery  applications.
